candidate_problems.text: How exactly should 'asymmetric feedback in record formation' be defined and quantified so that it adds explanatory content beyond standard low-entropy initial conditions plus coarse-graining? The conjecture risks redescribing the thermodynamic arrow unless it identifies a precise mechanism, measure, or theorem linking record stability, decoherence, memory, and causal asymmetry to temporal direction.

What selects or explains the special low-entropy boundary condition itself, and can this systems-level account do more than conditionally explain the arrow given that boundary? The major open tension is whether the conjecture explains time's direction or merely relocates the mystery to cosmological initial conditions.

Can the proposed alignment of entropy increase, record production, and causal asymmetry be derived as a necessary or generic feature of realistic many-body systems, or are there physically plausible regimes where these arrows come apart? Testing robustness against counterexamples—e.g. recoherence, fluctuation-dominated systems, closed finite systems, or alternative coarse-grainings—would determine whether the conjecture identifies a deep structural principle or only a contingent heuristic.
